msgs.text: This proof gives presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group. Is it correct?

I would **not certify it as a complete proof in its present form**. The proposed presentation is plausible, and I found no immediate contradiction in the tame and maximal pro‑2 reductions, but several load-bearing steps are asserted rather than proved. 

The main problems are:

1. **Propositions 5.7 and 5.9, pages 16–17.** The argument identifies the central coordinate of finite Heisenberg word evaluation with an Alexander–Whitney cellular cup product for the two-relator complex. That identification is the essential chain-level statement, but it is not actually constructed or verified for twisted coefficients. Showing that the sum of the two relator cells has zero boundary does not by itself establish identities (38)–(45).

2. **Lemma 6.3, page 21, contains a concrete gap.** Two equivariant classes having the same restriction to \(V\) and vanishing on the zero section can still differ by a filtration-one class
   \[
   \Gamma_\gamma,\qquad \gamma\in H^1(H,V^\vee).
   \]
   Zero-section normalization removes the inflated scalar ambiguity, but not this \(H^1\)-ambiguity. In the particular cases considered, one may be able to repair this by proving \(H^1(H,V^\vee)=0\), using projectivity in the ramified case and odd order in the unramified case. The sentence invoking “naturality of the split projections” is not a proof of that vanishing.

3. **Lemmas 6.2 and 6.10–6.11, pages 20–25.** The half-orbit equivariant extension is described as a transfer class, and its graph pullback is then declared to be the Evens norm. No explicit cocycle, correction functions \(m_c\), or comparison with the normalized Shapiro map is supplied. This is especially important because the argument insists that it computes the actual equivariant determinant class, not merely a quadratic form with the same polarization.

These omissions are decisive: Proposition 6.15—the local determinant Gauss-sum calculation—depends on them, and Proposition 6.15 is then used directly in formulas (98)–(100), which drive the finite-target induction and ultimately Theorem 1.2.

So the appropriate verdict is:

**The claimed presentation has not been disproved by the manuscript’s elementary checks, but the manuscript does not currently prove it.** The missing arguments may be repairable, but until the chain-level duality and equivariant determinant/Shapiro–Evens calculations are written out rigorously, the main theorem remains unverified.

Ok, please let us know what all to insert/modify in the proof, giving the exact contents of said insertions/modifications, in order to satisfy your critiques completely and make the proof complete and coorrect.
